data.title: Goldman Sachs sees almost 30% correction risk in stocks
data.description:
A pickup in economic policy uncertainty is contributing to an increase to almost 30% in the risk of a drawdown in U.S. stocks, according to Goldman Sachs.

The risk is below previous peaks, but well above its unconditional level reached in September, Goldman analysts said Wednesday. They retrieved the ~30% figure from their newly developed framework to estimate drawdown risk over different horizons. The most extreme outcomes have materialized only after the probability crossed 35%.

Most of the risk pick-up since September has been driven by business cycle scores which track U.S. growth, inflation and policy, followed by continued equity valuation expansion, GS said.

“Market variables have contributed the most to the increase in inflation momentum, with both commodity prices and U.S. breakeven inflation increasing over the past few months,” Andrea Ferrario, leading a team of Goldman analysts, said.

Since 1950, a three-month max drawdown greater than 10% or a 12-month max drawdown of more than 20% has occurred about 55% of the time if the drawdown risk was higher than 35%, the firm said. The risk shifts down to about 40% of the time if the drawdown probability was between 25% and 35% compared with an unconditional frequency of 16%.

The S&P 500 (SP500) in August underwent an 8.5% maximum peak-to-trough. An eventual recovery left the benchmark up 23% in 2024. The index (SP500) has risen 0.6% since the start of 2025 trade through Wednesday.

At the “epicenter” of a surge in policy uncertainty is global trade and the risk of U.S. tariffs, Ferrario said. U.S. President-elect Donald Trump has said he’ll direct his administration coming to Washington later this month to pursue additional tariffs on Chinese goods, and tariffs on products from other countries.

U.S. regulation and fiscal policy is also stoking drawdown risk, while European policy uncertainty at “all-time highs” has already clipped European equity relative valuations, Ferrario said.

“While the predictive power of policy uncertainty for equity drawdowns has been mixed, with elevated policy uncertainty and geopolitical risks there is potential for setbacks that are very difficult to anticipate,” he said.

Investors can track the S&P 500 (SP500) through ETFs such as: (SPY), (UPRO), (VOO), (SDS) and (IVV).

data.title: Nvidia highlights gen-AI opportunities and affordable supercomputing: Oppenheimer
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) highlighted opportunities to advance generative artificial intelligence across an array of industries, including enterprise, robotics and auto, at the Consumer Electronics Show this week in Las Vegas, according to Oppenheimer.

Keeping true to its roots, the Jensen Huang-led firm also revealed a refreshed slate of gaming GPUs, including the 5090, 5080, 5071 Ti and 5070, Oppenheimer noted.

"Blackwell AI accelerators remain supply constrained," said Oppenheimer analysts, led by Rick Schafer, in a Thursday investor note. "H200 will continue to contribute meaningfully this year (30% of mix by our work). GB200 in full production since December. Liquid cooling requirements are likely a near-term hurdle to adoption of some GB200 SKUs."

Nvidia also focused on its enterprise offerings, including a trio of AI models, dubbed NIM, NeMo and Nemotron. The Nemotron models are based on Meta Platforms (META) Llama large language model.

"Robotics and automotive are growing AI market opportunities," Schafer said. "Management highlighted the need for three computing systems in robotics/auto."

These included DGX servers to train, AGX computers to run simulations and a connecting system.

"Management also announced Project DIGITS, a small form-factor AI supercomputer that functions as a personal private cloud —targeted towards data scientists, ML researchers, students, and developers," Schafer added.

James Foord, Investing Group Leader for The Pragmatic Investor, said Digits could be the most important announcement from the event for Nvidia. The affordable supercomputer, at $3,000, could help democratize AI and possibly add billions in new revenue.

"The implications here for Nvidia are huge, and I believe the Digits could take the market by storm," Foord noted. "The value of AI is only now becoming apparent to us, and it is limited to a great extent to big corporations."

data.title: J&J gets Priority Review designation for nipocalimab BLA
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) has received Priority Review designation from the FDA for the Biologics License Application for its drug nipocalimab in the treatment of antibody-positive patients with generalized myasthenia gravis.

Nipocalimab was also recently granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation by the FDA for the treatment of adults with moderate-to-severe Sjögren's disease.

data.title: Super Micro Computer begins volume shipments of max-performance servers
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) has begun volume shipments of its max-performance servers optimized for demanding high-performance workloads, including large-scale AI and cluster-scale high-performance computing.

The company’s max-performance X14 systems support the Intel Xeon 6900 series processors with P-cores, which feature up to 128 performance cores per CPU, support for high bandwidth MRDIMMs up to 8800 MT/s, and built-in accelerators including the AI-specific Intel AMX.

"The systems now shipping in volume promise to unlock new capabilities and levels of performance for our customers around the world, featuring low latency, maximum I/O expansion providing high throughput with 256 performance cores per system, 12 memory channels per CPU with MRDIMM support, and high performance EDSFF storage options," said Charles Liang, president and CEO of Supermicro (NASDAQ:SMCI).

To maximize the performance and density potential of the latest systems, Supermicro is offering complete in-house developed liquid cooling solutions, including cold plates for CPUs, GPUs, memory, Cooling Distribution Units, Cooling Distribution Manifolds, hoses, connectors, and cooling towers.

data.title: Bond selloff eases ahead of key jobs report; Stock market shut for Carter's mourning
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields fell on Thursday in what will be a truncated session, as a bond selloff finally eased up. Meanwhile, stock index futures slipped, continuing Wall Street's seesaw start to 2025.

The stock market is closed for a national day of mourning for former U.S. President Jimmy Carter. The bond market will shut early at 1400 ET.

Benchmark S&P 500 futures (SPX) ended -0.20%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -0.13%, and Dow futures (INDU) -0.28%.

The U.S. 10-year yield (US10Y) was down 5 basis points to 4.66%. The instrument hit levels not seen since November 2023 on Wednesday. The shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 3 basis points to 4.27%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

"We expect trading activity to be light and liquidity to weaken (today), given U.S. equity markets are closed and U.S. bond markets close early for former President Carter’s funeral, and that the December employment report looms on Friday," JPMorgan's Jay Barry, Jason Hunter, and Phoebe White said on Wednesday.

U.S. equities whipsawed through a volatile day in the previous session, eventually ending mixed. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) added +0.16% to finish at 5,918.23 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed +0.25% to settle at 42,635.20 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slipped -0.06% to conclude at 19,478.88 points.

Concerns over the effects of incoming President Donald Trump's policies on inflation, along with a Federal Reserve signaling a slower pace of interest rate cuts has pressured sentiment in the fledgling new year.

The minutes of the Fed's December monetary policy committee meeting published yesterday confirmed that committee participants believed they were "at or near the point at which it would be appropriate to slow the pace of policy easing." The minutes also showed that there was "elevated uncertainty" among committee members "regarding specifics about the scope and timing of potential changes to trade, immigration, fiscal, and regulatory policies and their potential effects on the economy."

"The minutes to the December FOMC meeting provided little new information on the near-term path of policy, indicating that the decision to cut last month was 'finely balanced,'" Barry, Hunter, and White said.

"The minutes clarified that expected tariffs were one reason the staff revised their inflation forecast higher. Meanwhile, there was no discussion about the forward path of balance sheet policy, although it was noted that the average estimate of survey respondents for the timing of the end of balance sheet runoff shifted a bit later, to June 2025," they added.

Market participants will return on Friday with their attention squarely focused on the December U.S. nonfarm payrolls report. Data on the labor market has pointed to renewed resilience recently after significant cooling in the middle of last year, which has led to traders dialing back their Fed rate cut bets.

data.title: Fed's Collins backs more gradual approach to rate cuts; December cut was 'insurance'
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Boston President Susan Collins endorsed Thursday a more cautious strategy for future interest-rate cuts as the U.S economic outlook faces "considerable uncertainty."

Collins noted that inflation remains sticky and "I expect inflation in 2025 to run somewhat higher than I previously thought, with the risks likely having shifted to the upside," she said in remarks prepared for delivery at for a NAIOP Massachusett event.

And the incoming Trump administration and new Congress could steer the economy in new directions with their economic policies, though it is premature to predict the precise impact, she added.

"This context calls for a patient approach to policy – taking the time to fully assess available information and not over-reacting to individual data readings, as we calibrate policy meeting by meeting."

Her rate outlook aligns with the median projection in the Summary of Economic Projections by participants in the policy-setting Federal Open Market Committee (that is, a more gradual approach to monetary easing), "but there is no preset path, and a projection is certainly not a promise."

"In my view, policy is well positioned to adjust as required to evolving conditions – holding at the current level for longer if there is little further progress on inflation, or easing sooner if the need arises," she said.

The Boston Fed chief said that the December rate reduction "provided some additional insurance to preserve healthy labor market conditions while maintaining a restrictive policy stance that is still needed to sustainably restore price stability.”

Going forward, with economic uncertainty high, "additional information– about developments in inflation and the labor market, about possible fiscal and trade policy changes, and more – will be key to determining the appropriate course for monetary policy in the year ahead," she said.

Earlier, Philadelphia Fed President Patrick Harker says he still sees the FOMC on a downward policy rate path.

data.title: Prysmian CEO says considering listing in the US - FT
data.description:
Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMY) is considering a dual listing in the US as the Italian cable company looks to do more acquisitions.

“We are seriously considering a dual listing,” Prysmian CEO Massimo Battaini told the Financial Times in a story published on Thursday.

Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMY) wants to expand in the US after agreeing to purchase Encore Wire for $4 billion in April.

Battaini expects a US IPO to take about a year to complete and said acquisitions will be centered on areas where the company is weaker, such as in the telecommunications sector.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed's Harker still sees rates on downward path
data.description:
Philadelphia Fed Patrick Harker says he still sees the Federal Open Market Committee on a downward policy rate path. But the economy remains "in very unsettled times." There remain "numerous upside risks," he added, according to his prepared remarks.

As the FOMC's minutes from its last meeting described, the Fed officials are likely to be more cautious in their easing. "The exact speed I continue to go along this path will be fully dependent upon the incoming data," Harker said in his prepared text.

He's optimistic about the U.S. economy. "The overall underpinnings of our economy remain strong," he said. He's particularly encouraged that "shelter disinflation has finally enmeshed itself into the monthly readings after being the upside outlier for many, many months."

Tempering that optimism is an increase in mortgage delinquencies, he added.

Harker, who will retire from the Philly Fed on June 30, 2025, is not a voting member of the FOMC this year.

The delivered remarks will be followed by a Q&A segment.

Breaking news... check back for updates.

data.title: BP to boost oil output from India's largest field by 44%, ONGC says
data.description:
BP (NYSE:BP) plans to increase oil production at India's largest field by 44% and gas production by 89%, state-controlled Oil & Natural Gas Corp. said Thursday.

The comments came a day after ONGC selected BP (BP) as its technical service provider to help boost production at its flagship Mumbai High field from a baseline of 45.47M metric tons of crude and 70.4B cm of gas.

BP (BP) plans to raise oil production by 44% to 65.41M tons and gas output by 89% to 112.63B cm from the Mumbai High asset beginning in the next fiscal year starting April 1, with full-scale visibility anticipated by 2027-28, ONGC said.

The incremental production is forecast to generate up to $10.3B of additional oil and gas revenue for India, and contributions from royalties and other levies totaling as much as $5B, the company said.

The Mumbai High field off India's west coast reached peak production of 471K bbl/day of oil in March 1985, but declined to 134K bbl/day in April 2024, a tender document showed last year.

data.title: Italian PM Meloni denies discussing Starlink deal with Musk
data.description:
Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni on Thursday refuted claims that she has discussed any potential deal for encrypted communications to be used in the country's military with Starlink (STRLK) (SPACE) owner Elon Musk.

Meloni has maintained a favorable relationship and met with Musk during a surprise visit to Donald Trump's Mar-a-Lago residence over the weekend ahead of Trump's second presidential term.

However, she has strongly rejected claims of reaching a telecommunication services deal and said she is only evaluating options.

"The prime minister's office categorically denies, considering it ridiculous, that SpaceX was a topic during the meeting with U.S. President-elect Donald Trump," the Italian PM's office said.

"We are simply in the investigative phase, which is why I don't understand all the accusations that have been made. I'll tell you more: I don't even personally have clear ideas on this matter," Meloni reportedly said at a press conference.

A Bloomberg report from Sunday suggested Italy was in talks for a $1.65B deal with Starlink.

Musk on Monday posted on his platform X that he was "ready to provide Italy the most secure and advanced connectivity!"

Italy's defense minister, Guido Crosetto, told parliament on Wednesday his office had not signed or agreed on any contracts with Musk's company.

Starlink, a unit of SpaceX, currently has 6,700 low-Earth orbit satellites active in space, nearly two-thirds of all active satellites in place. Last year, it added more than 20 countries to its customer base, providing services in more than 100 countries and territories.
